title: Design a Distributed Rate Limiter
description: Protect your APIs from being overwhelmed by too many requests. Learn Token Bucket and Sliding Window algorithms.

steps.detail: Drag the API Gateway onto the canvas. It usually acts as the entry point for rate limiting.

explanation.what: A centralized entry point for all API requests. It handles cross-cutting concerns like authentication and, importantly, rate limiting.
explanation.why: Rate limiting at the gateway level prevents malicious or excessive traffic from even reaching your backend services, saving compute resources.
explanation.tradeoff: Adds a small amount of latency to every request. If the gateway goes down, your entire API is unreachable.
explanation.realWorld: AWS API Gateway and Nginx both provide built-in rate limiting modules used by thousands of companies.
explanation.capacity: Modern API Gateways can handle 50k+ RPS per instance.

steps.instruction: Add a Rate Limiter Logic Node
steps.detail: In this simulation, we'll use a specialized Rate Limiter component.

explanation.what: The component that actually implements the counting logic (e.g., Token Bucket).
explanation.why: Separating rate limiting logic allows you to fine-tune algorithms without redeploying your entire gateway.
explanation.tradeoff: Adds an extra internal network hop.
explanation.realWorld: Stripe uses a library called 'redis-rate-limiter' to handle their massive volume of payment requests.

steps.detail: Drag a Cache (Redis) and connect it to the Rate Limiter.

explanation.what: Stores the current state of counters (e.g., how many tokens are left for a specific IP).
explanation.why: Standard in-memory counters won't work in a distributed system because requests from the same user might hit different servers. Redis provides atomic operations (INCR) to keep counters consistent.
explanation.tradeoff: The 'thundering herd' problem if Redis is slow. All requests will wait on the Redis check.
explanation.realWorld: GitHub's API rate limits are tracked in a dedicated Redis cluster to ensure consistency across their global infrastructure.
explanation.capacity: Redis can handle ~100k atomic operations per second on a single core.

questions.question: Which algorithm is best for allowing short bursts of traffic while maintaining a steady average rate?

explanation.correct: Token Bucket allows for bursts up to the bucket size. Tokens accumulate when traffic is low, and can be used all at once for a burst.

wrongAnswers.a: Fixed Window allows 'double' traffic at the boundary of windows.
wrongAnswers.c: Leaky Bucket shapes traffic to a strictly constant rate, forbidding bursts.
wrongAnswers.d: Sliding Window is accurate but doesn't handle bursts as naturally as Token Bucket.
